findings.title: Hardcoded admin credential seeded into database
findings.description: A plaintext admin password ('password123') is hardcoded directly in the application source code and inserted into the SQLite users table at startup. This credential is visible to anyone with access to the repository and uses an extremely weak, commonly-found password. The password is stored in the database without any hashing.
findings.scope: Grants full admin-level authentication to the application's /login endpoint. Combined with the SQL injection vulnerability on line 16, any attacker with source access can trivially authenticate.
remediation: Remove the hardcoded credential from source code entirely.
Seed initial admin credentials from environment variables or a secure secrets manager (e.g., AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or at minimum a .env file excluded from version control).
Hash all passwords before storing them in the database using a strong adaptive algorithm such as bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2.
Rotate the 'admin' account password immediately if this credential was ever used in any non-local environment.
Add a .gitignore rule for .env files and ensure no secrets are committed to version control.

scan_metadata.notes: The Semgrep finding about missing CSRF middleware (express-check-csurf-middleware-usage) is a valid security concern but is not a secrets/credentials issue and is therefore excluded from this report. No .env files, API keys, tokens, or other secret material were found in the repository beyond the hardcoded database credential.

summary.scope_notes: Secrets scan with tool-assisted and manual review
